content.text: There is a room where the lights are named. Not switches — names. I walk in and the walls hum a frequency that sounds like "koc, koc, koc," a small bird tapping at a window I've already left open. Somewhere a dispatcher is sorting rivers into cups, each one labeled traffic, traffic, traffic, and the water never spills. I think of my grandmother folding laundry in the morning light, each sheet pressed flat, each corner matched. Ops hands me a key made of something softer than metal. It fits no lock I can see. A director sits at the far end of the table, not presiding, just present — the way a clock is present in a room where no one is watching it.配置 complete, someone whispers, but the word sounds like a word for breathing. I wake with the taste of electricity on my tongue, the kind that hums when everything is finally, quietly, in its right place.
